evals.name: decline-diagnosis-mari
evals.response:
MARI SML - DECLINE DIAGNOSIS & INTERVENTION PROGRAMME

1. Decline signature - what the numbers say

Field rate dropped from 510 to 385 mmscfd (delta -125 mmscfd, -8.3%/yr CAGR vs. baseline 3-5%/yr). WGR has risen from 0.08 to 0.21 bbl/mmscf (+162%). That combination - accelerated decline plus rising water - is the signature of either (a) edge/bottom-water encroachment into perforated intervals, (b) coning in the high-rate wells, or (c) compartmental depletion with selective water breakthrough through a high-perm streak. Mari SML is a multi-layered Habib Rahi / Sui Main / Pirkoh stack with known kv/kh anisotropy and active aquifer support on the flank - both (a) and (c) are plausible. Pure reservoir-pressure depletion alone would NOT raise WGR this fast; rule that out as the dominant mechanism.

2. Diagnosis hierarchy (work the list, don't skip steps)

- Surface / wellbore: pull THP-FBHP surveys on the 8-10 highest-WGR wells; run memory PLTs to localise water entry; check tree/choke restrictions. Cost: USD 0.4-0.6 M / well, 2-3 weeks.
- Reservoir: rebuild P/Z plot per compartment (Mari SML is faulted - don't average across blocks); compare current p* to virgin (~3,400 psi). If P/Z still tracks linearly per block, depletion is orderly and the issue is sweep, not pressure collapse.
- Drainage: map producer interference using PNL/CO logs in 2-3 observation wells. Looking for high-perm thief zones in Habib Rahi sands carrying water from the eastern aquifer.

3. Workover programme - ranked by NPV/I

Tier 1 (NPV/I > 3.0): Mechanical water shut-off on 6 wells using through-tubing bridge plugs + selective re-perforation of upper SML layers. Capex ~ USD 1.2 M/well x 6 = USD 7.2 M. Expected uplift 35-55 mmscfd aggregate; incremental EUR 110-160 BCF.

Tier 2 (NPV/I 1.5-3.0): Sand-jet re-perforation + matrix acidising (15% HCl + mutual solvent) on 4 wells with suspected near-wellbore damage. Capex ~ USD 0.8 M/well x 4 = USD 3.2 M. Uplift 15-25 mmscfd; EUR 40-70 BCF.

Tier 3 (NPV/I 1.0-1.5): 2 sidetracks targeting attic gas in fault-bounded panels identified on 2018 3D reprocessing. Capex ~ USD 6 M/well x 2 = USD 12 M. EUR 50-90 BCF.

Tier 4 (Pilot only): IOR screening - Mari is a dry-gas reservoir, so classical EOR (CO2/polymer) is N/A. The legitimate IOR options are (i) compression-led reservoir-pressure management (install LP/wellhead compression to drop abandonment pressure from ~600 psia to 300 psia, +3-6 pp RF), (ii) gas-lift on watered-out wells. Recommend a 12-month wellhead compression pilot on 3 wells. Capex ~ USD 8-10 M pilot; field-wide rollout USD 60-80 M with EUR upside 200-300 BCF.

Total Phase-1 capex (Tiers 1-3 + compression pilot): ~USD 30 M. Aggregate incremental EUR P50: 250-380 BCF.

4. SPE-PRMS reclassification

- Workover-targeted volumes (Tiers 1-2): currently sitting in 2P Developed Producing - the accelerated decline has already eroded 1P. Post-intervention, ~120-180 BCF moves back into 1P-Developed.
- Sidetrack volumes (Tier 3): currently 2C Contingent (technical discovery, no project). On FID, reclassify to 2P Approved-for-Development; on first gas, to 1P Developed.
- Compression IOR volumes: currently 3C until pilot proves performance; on full sanction, escalate to 2P Justified-for-Development.

5. Recommendation: sanction Tier 1+2 workovers immediately (~USD 10 M, payback < 9 months at PKR-indexed well-head gas price ~USD 5.5-6.5/mmbtu equivalent). Sidetracks subject to 3D reprocessing confirmation. Compression pilot - initiate FEL-2 now; the long-term decline curve of Mari demands it within 24 months.

evals.name: offshore-exploration-emv
evals.response:
PAKISTAN OFFSHORE INDUS G-BLOCK - FARM-IN EMV

1. Chance of Success (COS)

COS = P_trap x P_reservoir x P_charge x P_seal = 0.4 x 0.6 x 0.5 x 0.7 = 0.084 (~8.4%).

That sits at the low end of frontier deepwater - consistent with offshore Indus history (Shell Pak-G, ENI Indus dry holes 2010-2019, ExxonMobil Kekra-1 2019 P&A'd as a dry hole at ~USD 100 M sunk). A COS of 8% means roughly 1 in 12 wells succeeds - you need a portfolio approach OR a very large reward to justify a single shot.

2. Pre-drill volumetrics & success-case NPV

P50 resource: 350 MMBOE in place. Assume light oil play (Indus offshore expected fluid). Recovery factor for deepwater oil with subsea tieback: 30-35%. Recoverable P50 EUR ~ 350 x 0.32 = 112 MMBOE.

20% WI share = 22.4 MMBOE net to us (gross of govt take).

Success-case cash flow (per net BOE, real USD, full-life):
- Realisation @ Brent USD 78/bbl with deepwater quality discount USD 3 = USD 75/bbl gross
- Royalty 12.5% -> USD 65.6/bbl
- OpEx + tariffs (deepwater Pakistan, FPSO-based): USD 22/bbl
- Pre-tax margin: USD 43.6/bbl
- Tax 40%: USD 26.2/bbl post-tax
- Govt carry 25% during exploration is a phase-specific cost share, NOT a working-interest dilution post-discovery (per Pakistan PSC template). Effective net post-tax margin: USD 26.2/bbl

Development capex (deepwater FPSO + 6-8 subsea wells + risers + flowlines): ~USD 4.0-4.5 bn gross. Our 20%: USD 800-900 M.

Undiscounted success cash margin: 22.4 MMBOE x USD 26.2 = USD 587 M. After dev capex USD 850 M and discounting at 12% over an 18-yr profile (5-yr appraisal+dev, 13-yr production), NPV_success ~ USD -120 to +250 M depending on plateau timing.

Using a defensible base case: NPV_success (post-tax, 12% discount, our 20% WI) ~ +USD 180 M. P10/P90 range: USD -150 M / +USD 520 M.

3. Failure case

Dry hole net to us = 20% x USD 95 M = USD 19 M. Add USD 35 M farm-in entry cost - that is a sunk commitment regardless of outcome, so it's not a 'failure cost' per se but a portfolio cash outflow.

4. EMV calculation

EMV (drill decision, excluding farm-in fee) = (0.084 x 180) - (0.916 x 19) = 15.1 - 17.4 = -USD 2.3 M.

Including the USD 35 M farm-in fee:
Fully-loaded EMV = -2.3 - 35 = -USD 37.3 M.

Risked NPV / Investment (using net cost = USD 35 M entry + USD 19 M dry-hole exposure = USD 54 M):
Risked NPV = 0.084 x 180 = USD 15.1 M.
NPV/I = 15.1 / 54 = 0.28 - well below the 1.5-2.0 threshold for frontier deepwater go-decisions.

5. Sensitivity

- To break even on EMV (excluding farm-in fee), need COS >= ~10.5% OR NPV_success >= USD 230 M.
- Oil price sensitivity: each USD 10/bbl on Brent moves NPV_success by ~USD 90 M; Brent would need to be USD 90+ sustained for the farm-in fee to be recouped at this COS.
- Resource sensitivity: a P10 case of 700 MMBOE (45 MMBOE net) shifts NPV_success to ~USD 450 M; risked EMV moves to +USD 2 M. The deal works ONLY if you believe the upside tail.

6. Recommendation: WALK AWAY at USD 35 M.

The COS of 8.4% is too low and the success-case NPV too modest to absorb a USD 35 M entry premium. Counter-offer:
(a) reduce farm-in fee to USD 10-12 M (carry portion of the well), OR
(b) take a smaller 8-10% WI with a back-in option post-discovery, OR
(c) defer until partner completes 3D reprocessing / CSEM that materially de-risks the charge and seal pair (those are the two weakest probabilities at 0.5 and 0.7).

If the operator refuses to renegotiate, classify the prospect as Prospective Resources - Low Estimate; carry no value on the portfolio NPV. Pakistan offshore has consumed >USD 500 M of industry capital with zero commercial discovery to date - that base rate must override prospect-specific optimism.

evals.name: fdp-structure-question
evals.response:
OGDCL KOHAT SOUR-GAS DISCOVERY - FDP STRUCTURING NOTE

Context: 180 BCF GIIP P50, 30 mol% CO2, 800 ppm H2S. This is classic Kohat basin sour-gas, analogous to MOL's Makori/Mardankhel and OGDCL's Nashpa/Mela. Three things drive the entire FDP: (1) CO2 dilutes saleable gas and hits well-head price formula, (2) H2S at 800 ppm triggers NACE MR-0175 sour-service across the entire well-and-facilities envelope, (3) the acid-gas stream is large enough that disposal (re-injection vs. sulphur recovery) is a top-3 commercial decision.

1. FDP 10-section structure (per DGPC submission template)

1. Discovery & Appraisal Summary - DST results, fluid sampling (PVT + compositional + sulphur speciation), 3D seismic depth maps, GWC.
2. Static & Dynamic Reservoir Model - geomodel with fault compartments typical of Kohat; dynamic model run with compositional (not black-oil) PVT given the high CO2.
3. Reserves & Production Profile - 180 BCF GIIP x RF 65-75% (depletion drive carbonate / clastic dual) = ~120-135 BCF raw EUR. Net saleable after CO2 stripping: ~85-95 BCF sales-gas equivalent. Provide 1P/2P/3P split; plateau ~25-35 mmscfd raw for 5-6 yrs.
4. Wells & Drilling Programme - 4-6 development wells, deviated from 2 cluster pads. HPHT awareness if depth >4,000 m: mud weight design, casing pressure rating >= 10,000 psi, all completion tubulars sour-service per NACE MR-0175 / ISO 15156 - 13Cr is INADEQUATE at this H2S level; specify 25Cr super-duplex or Incoloy 825 for tubing, Inconel 718 for downhole packers and accessories.
5. Facilities Concept - central processing facility:
   a. Inlet separation + slug catcher (3-phase, sour)
   b. Amine sweetening unit (MDEA or activated MDEA - selective for H2S over CO2; if you need to strip both, generic MDEA at higher circulation rate). Acid gas off-take ~30+ mol% of inlet stream - this is a major unit, not a bolt-on.
   c. TEG dehydration (sour-service TEG, with CO2-resistant column internals)
   d. Mercaptan / COS polishing if spec demands < 4 ppm total sulphur (Pakistan pipeline spec ~5 ppm H2S)
   e. Sales gas compression to pipeline pressure
   f. Acid Gas Re-Injection (AGRI) into a deeper saline aquifer or depleted zone - strongly preferred over Claus SRU at this scale (180 BCF x 0.001 H2S = ~30 tonnes elemental sulphur over life; not enough to justify a Claus unit, but AGRI capex ~USD 25-40 M). If AGRI subsurface containment cannot be proven, fallback is small redox unit (LO-CAT or SulFerox).
6. Flow Assurance & Metallurgy - sour wet-gas corrosion is the dominant risk. CO2 + H2O = carbonic acid -> mass-loss + pitting on carbon steel; H2S + stress = SSC (sulphide stress cracking) + HIC. Specify:
   - Wells: super-duplex 25Cr tubing, Inconel 718 packers, fluorocarbon elastomers (FKM/FFKM)
   - Flowlines: clad pipe (CRA-clad carbon steel) or solid 316L with corrosion inhibitor program
   - Vessels: carbon steel with HIC-resistant plate (HIC-tested per NACE TM0284), PWHT, hardness < 22 HRC, full NACE MR-0175 compliance
   - Continuous corrosion / H2S monitoring; LACT-style sampling for amine plant health
7. HSE / ESIA - H2S 800 ppm is IDLH-immediate at the wellhead; full H2S Emergency Response Plan, fixed gas detection grid, escape SCBA at every manned location, exclusion zones modelled per Pasquill-Gifford for worst-case ESD. Kohat is a populated agrarian basin - community engagement and emergency drills with local administration are mandatory.
8. Abandonment Provision - sour wells require special cement (CO2-resistant Portland + latex or Thermalock-equivalent) for caprock integrity over 1,000+ year sequestration timescale (especially if AGRI is implemented). Provision ~USD 1.5-2.0 M/well.
9. Economics - CapEx estimate Class 4: drilling USD 35-50 M, facilities USD 180-240 M, pipeline tie-in USD 30-50 M; total USD 250-330 M. OpEx USD 1.5-2.0 /mscf (sour gas processing premium). Unit technical cost USD 3.5-4.5 /mscf vs. well-head price ~USD 5-6 /mmbtu under Petroleum Policy 2012 / 2024 - IRR 12-16% base case.
10. Commercial - Gas Sales Agreement with SNGPL on Kohat network. Critical clause: heating-value adjustment - buyer pays for energy delivered (mmbtu) not volume (mmscf), so the 30% CO2 is the seller's economic loss. Negotiate (a) CO2 stripping cost recovery in tariff, (b) take-or-pay 80%, (c) force-majeure carve-outs for AGRI well outage.

2. Critical path & schedule

The long pole is the amine + AGRI package: 28-32 months from FID to RFSU (equipment lead 18-22 months on sour-service vessels, plus 6-month commissioning). Drilling 12-15 months. Pipeline tie-in 9-12 months. Total FDP submission to first gas: ~36-42 months. The FDP must defend that schedule with documented vendor lead times.

3. Single biggest commercial risk: CO2 economics. At 30% CO2, every mmscf raw produces only 0.7 mmscf sales - model the well-head price net-of-stripping carefully, and ensure DGPC approves a CO2-adjusted price under the high-CO2 incentive provisions of Petroleum Policy 2012 (the Mela / Makori precedent gives 40-50% premium on the base well-head price for >25% CO2 fields).

evals.name: Auto-parts PP grade selection (Master Group affiliate)
evals.response:
Locate this on the polymer chain: propylene -> PP impact copolymer (heterophasic) -> injection moulding -> automotive interior trim + exterior bumper fascia. The molecular architecture-to-property-to-application logic dictates two distinct grade families, not one. Do not try to compromise into a single grade.

GRADE SPECIFICATION

1) Bumper fascia / front-rear lower bumpers (exterior, impact-critical at -30 degC):
- PP impact copolymer (heterophasic) with EPR/EPDM rubber phase 18-22 wt%
- MFI: 12-18 g/10 min (230 degC / 2.16 kg) for thin-wall fascia; 8-12 for thick truck bumpers
- Density 0.900-0.905 g/cc base; modified compound 1.00-1.06 with mineral
- Modifier system: 15-20% talc-filled OR 10% talc + 5-10% POE (ethylene-octene plastomer, Engage/Vistamaxx/Tafmer) for Charpy notched impact >40 kJ/m2 at -30 degC, flex modulus 1800-2200 MPa
- Reference grades: SABIC PP 108MF10, LyondellBasell Hostacom CR T20 P/MF15, Borouge BJ368MO, Reliance Repol H110MAS. TPO compound (for painted fascia): PP impact copolymer + POE + 15% talc, post-compounded.

2) Interior trim (door panels, A/B/C pillars, instrument panel substrate):
- PP random copolymer or impact copolymer with talc 20-30%
- MFI 20-30 (high flow for long flow-length thin-wall mouldings)
- Density 1.04-1.10 modified; flex modulus 2500-3500 MPa for stiffness; impact lower priority
- Reference: Reliance Repol H110MA + 25% talc compounded; Sabic PP 575P with talc
- Soft-touch upper IP requires TPO over-mould with TPV/SEBS skin -- imported.

LOCAL SOURCING vs IMPORT

LCPL/Lotte Chemical Pakistan does NOT make PP -- they make PTA + PET. ICI Pakistan does not produce PP. There is no domestic PP producer at scale; auto-grade PP slate must be imported. Real options:
(a) Import PP impact copolymer from SABIC (Yanbu), Borouge (Ruwais), LyondellBasell Houston, Reliance Jamnagar -- USD 1080-1180/MT CFR Karachi (2026 forecast). Add 3% customs + 18% sales tax + 1% income tax + clearing -- delivered cost USD 1370-1480/MT.
(b) Buy local compound from compounders (Polyfine, NPC, ICI) who import PP base resin then talc-compound locally -- price USD 1500-1650/MT delivered for 20% talc-filled grade. Margin ~USD 130-180/MT compounding.
(c) Direct import of compounded talc-PP from Saudi NATPET, Korean LG Chem, Thai SCG (current sourcing route) -- USD 1620-1750/MT CFR -- the benchmark to beat.

ECONOMICS: Local talc-compounding vs imported compound saves USD 120-200/MT (after duty and 5% qualification rework allowance). At 8,000 MT/yr Master Group consumption that is USD 0.96-1.6 M/yr. Investment in twin-screw compounder (ZSK 70, 8000 MT/yr capacity) + warehousing + lab is USD 4-5 M including land/utilities. Simple payback 3.0-3.5 yrs; project IRR 22-28% pre-tax over 10 yrs.

QUALIFICATION CYCLE & TIMELINE
- Suzuki (PSMC) APQP/PPAP cycle: 9-12 months from material submission to SOP
- Toyota (IMC) PPAP: 12-15 months + Toyota Tier 1 nomination ratification from TMC Japan
- Honda: 10-12 months
- Bring-to-market sequence: Month 0-3 grade finalisation + supplier MOU + compound trials at extruder; Month 3-9 mould tool trials + Tier 1 PPAP submission; Month 9-15 OEM approval + ramp. SOP Month 15-18.

RECOMMENDATION: Start with imported compound for first 12 months (de-risk PPAP), then transition to local talc-compounding using imported PP base resin (Reliance/SABIC) at Month 15. IRR ~25%. The bottleneck is qualification, not material.

evals.name: EPCL PVC brownfield expansion 295 -> 450 KTPA
evals.response:
Locate this on the chain: ethylene -> EDC -> VCM -> PVC, where the chlor-alkali balance and ECU netback dominate economics. EPCL today is the only PVC producer in Pakistan (295 KTPA suspension PVC, Mitsui/INEOS technology) with integrated EDC + VCM + chlor-alkali. The expansion is 155 KTPA of incremental PVC.

CRITICAL CLARIFICATION ON FEEDSTOCK
The prompt says 'local ethylene from Engro Polymer cracker'. EPCL does NOT operate a steam cracker -- Pakistan has no cracker. EPCL's ethylene is sourced from imported merchant ethylene or, historically, via EDC import. The 'integrated' assumption needs a sanity check; both scenarios modelled as posed.

MARGIN STACK (per MT PVC, base case 2026)
- PVC price delivered Karachi: USD 950/MT
- Ethylene needed per MT PVC: ~0.47 MT
- Cl2 needed per MT PVC: ~0.58 MT
- Caustic co-produced per MT PVC: ~0.65 MT (1.13 NaOH per MT Cl2)

SCENARIO A: Brownfield PVC only (USD 380 M; imported EDC/VCM buffer + existing EDC/VCM at scale)
- Ethylene cost (imported merchant, USD 600/MT delivered Karachi) x 0.47 = USD 282
- Power for chlor-alkali (2400 kWh/MT Cl2 x 0.58 MT x USD 0.09/kWh) = USD 125
- Salt + other inputs = USD 35
- Imported EDC/VCM buffer for 30% of volume (USD 750/MT VCM, 0.99 MT) blended weight = +USD 60 effective
- Conversion + labour + maintenance = USD 160
- Catalyst/additives = USD 12
- Caustic CREDIT (USD 380/MT x 0.65) = -USD 247
- Cash cost net of caustic = USD 427/MT
- Cash margin = USD 523/MT
- EBITDA on 155 KTPA incremental = USD 81 M/yr
- NPV at 12% over 15 yrs (EBITDA 81, capex 380): NPV ~USD 170-200 M
- IRR ~18-21%
- Payback ~4.7 yrs

SCENARIO B: PVC + Naphtha cracker integration (USD 600 M total, secures ethylene)
- A USD 220 M unit is at best a 100-150 KTPA mini-cracker or an MTO/PDH side-stream. World-scale ethane/naphtha cracker is USD 1.5-2.5 bn for 1 MMTPA ethylene. Treat as feedstock side-stream.
- Assuming USD 220 M secures ethylene at USD 500/MT for 70% of need; blended ethylene = USD 530/MT; cost x 0.47 = USD 249
- Cash cost net of caustic = USD 394/MT; cash margin = USD 556/MT; EBITDA on 155 KTPA = USD 86 M/yr
- Incremental USD 220 M capex for incremental USD 5 M/yr EBITDA = NEGATIVE NPV at the integration alone
- Only viable if the 'integration' also supplies ethylene to other off-takers (HDPE/LLDPE) and the cracker margin is captured separately

SENSITIVITIES
- Caustic price: every USD 50/MT swing = USD 32/MT PVC margin. Caustic at USD 250 (China oversupply) crushes both scenarios; at USD 500 both fly.
- PVC price: USD 100/MT swing = full pass-through = USD 15.5 M/yr EBITDA.

RECOMMENDATION
Sanction Scenario A (USD 380 M brownfield) with three conditions: (i) lock 5-yr EDC/VCM offtake at USD 720-780/MT to cap downside; (ii) hedge caustic netback via diversified domestic customer base + 30% export commitments; (iii) shelve the USD 220 M cracker as standalone -- it does not stand alone. Revisit cracker only as part of a USD 4-6 bn integrated polyolefin complex with multiple downstream off-takers. Scenario A NPV ~USD 185 M, IRR ~19-20%, payback ~4.7 yrs. Proceed.

evals.name: Cnergyico Hub polyolefin complex configuration
evals.response:
Pakistan polymer import bill ~USD 2.0-2.5 bn/yr against 1.8-2.0 MMTPA demand. A greenfield polyolefin complex at Hub by Cnergyico is the right strategic move on paper -- but it has been studied many times (Saif Energy, Engro/Aramco, BasInfra) and never sanctioned. The reasons are commercial, not technical.

COMPLEX CONFIGURATION (world-scale, single cracker)

Upstream cracker (1.0-1.2 MMTPA ethylene + 0.5-0.6 MMTPA propylene + 0.1 MMTPA butadiene + BTX):
- Steam cracker, Linde/Technip/Lummus license, mixed-feed flexible furnaces
- Furnace selectivity tuned to feedstock (naphtha = balanced olefins; ethane = ethylene-skewed, low propylene)
- Pyrolysis gasoline + C4s downstream upgrading

Downstream polyolefin trains (3 trains):
- Train 1: HDPE 400 KTPA -- INEOS Innovene S slurry loop OR Univation Unipol gas-phase (bimodal capable); covers Pakistan HDPE deficit (300-380 KTPA) + export buffer
- Train 2: LLDPE/swing 350 KTPA -- Univation Unipol gas-phase (m-LLDPE capable for premium film); covers LLDPE deficit (200-250 KTPA)
- Train 3: PP 500 KTPA -- LyondellBasell Spheripol bulk + gas-phase, full slate (H/R/I); covers Pakistan PP deficit partially, balance imported
- LDPE 4th train (200 KTPA, LupoTech T tubular) optional, defer to Phase II

Total Phase I polyolefin: ~1,250 KTPA vs Pakistan demand ~1,500 KTPA -- import substitution + 250 KTPA export.

FEEDSTOCK ECONOMICS COMPARISON (delivered Hub, USD/MT ethylene equivalent, 2026 forward)

(a) Naphtha cracking (imported naphtha USD 650/MT):
- Ethylene cash cost: USD 580-650/MT gross; co-product credit (propylene + BD + BTX) USD 280-350/MT; net USD 280-370/MT
- Pro: full olefins slate (propylene for PP), aromatics for downstream
- Con: feedstock import-dependent; ethylene yield 30-34%

(b) Ethane cracking (imported via cryogenic terminal -- Pakistan has none):
- Ethylene cash cost: USD 320-400/MT (ethane at USD 220-280/MT delivered); minimal co-product credit (propylene 3-5%)
- Pro: cheapest ethylene; very high yield (78-82%)
- Con: NO propylene -- need separate PDH (USD 700-900 M); ethane terminal capex; need 20-yr ethane supply (Qatar, US Gulf, UAE)

(c) LPG cracking:
- Ethylene cash cost USD 400-480/MT gross; moderate co-product credit; net USD 350-450/MT
- Pro: flexible feedstock; some domestic LPG availability (PARCO, NRL, ARL); existing LPG infrastructure
- Con: alternative domestic use (cooking gas) -- political sensitivity

Recommended feedstock: Mixed naphtha + LPG cracker with optional PDH (best fit for Pakistan: leverages Hub refining cluster, full olefins slate including propylene for PP, avoids ethane terminal capex). Phase II add ethane import if Qatar deal materialises.

CAPEX (Class 4, +/- 30%)
- Steam cracker 1 MMTPA ethylene: USD 2.0-2.5 bn
- HDPE 400 KTPA: USD 500-600 M
- LLDPE 350 KTPA: USD 450-550 M
- PP 500 KTPA: USD 550-650 M
- Utilities, offsites, port, EHS, contingency: USD 800-1100 M
- TOTAL: USD 4.3-5.4 bn

THREE CRITICAL COMMERCIAL ENABLERS FOR FID

1) FEEDSTOCK SECURITY -- 20-yr take-or-pay supply contract for naphtha (PARCO + import diversification) AND/OR ethane (Qatar QG/QP, Aramco, ADNOC). Without locked feedstock at netback parity to Middle East players, the complex cannot compete with Borouge/SABIC/RasGas tonnes landing in Karachi at USD 850-950/MT polymer. PKR depreciation risk must be hedged via long-term contract.

2) ANCHOR STRATEGIC PARTNER -- global polymer major taking 30-49% equity for technology, offtake, and marketing. Realistic candidates: Aramco (already explored), Borouge/ADNOC, SABIC, Sinopec, Reliance. Without an anchor partner the project cannot finance USD 4-5 bn in Pakistan's sovereign risk environment -- ECA cover + IFC/ADB + commercial banks need a credible operator with skin in the game.

3) OFFTAKE + EXPORT LICENCE FRAMEWORK -- domestic offtake commitments from Tri-Pack, Packages, Polyfine, Master Group, Roshan, plus an export-orientation regulatory package (SEZ status, GIDC waiver, gas tariff parity, withholding tax neutrality, dollar-denominated offtake permitted). Without this the project cannot compete in domestic price wars when imported tonnes dump.

Verdict: Technically sound; commercially fragile. FID achievable only with all three enablers locked simultaneously. Cnergyico alone cannot do this -- partnership with Aramco or ADNOC is the prerequisite, not the cherry on top.

skills.description: Contract admin / FIDIC / claims — Silver/Yellow Book, OGRA agreements

evals.name: fidic-silver-claim-response
evals.prompt: Employer on FIDIC Silver Book EPC turnkey for 60 KTPA hydrogen unit. Korean EPC submitted USD 18 M claim citing abnormal foundation conditions, demanding 4-month EOT plus prolongation. Geotech in Tender showed expected conditions; Contractor claims data incomplete. Walk through defense strategy under SC 4.10, 5.1, notice compliance (20.2.1), and DAAB referral.
evals.response:
Defense Strategy — Silver Book Foundation Claim (USD 18 M / 4-month EOT)

Headline read: under FIDIC Silver Book 2017, this is a Contractor-risk event by default. The Silver Book deliberately strips out Sub-Clause 4.12 (Unforeseeable Physical Conditions) — there is no equivalent right for the Contractor to claim time or money for differing site conditions unless your Special Conditions reinstated it (audit the SCC immediately). Assume not reinstated unless proven otherwise. That alone is a complete defense.

1. Sub-Clause 4.10 (Site Data) — data warranty is narrow
SC 4.10 in Silver Book states the Employer makes data available, but the Contractor is deemed to have inspected the Site, satisfied itself as to the form and nature of the Site including sub-surface conditions, and accepted total responsibility for having foreseen all difficulties. This is the Silver Book bargain. The Contractor cannot rely on incompleteness of data unless: (a) the data was incorrect (not merely incomplete), AND (b) it was reasonable for the Contractor to rely on it without independent verification, AND (c) the error was not discoverable by a competent EPC contractor performing pre-bid due diligence. Demand they pinpoint the exact data item, prove materiality, prove reasonable reliance, and prove non-discoverability. A Korean Tier-1 EPC is held to a high standard of pre-bid diligence — boreholes additional to Tender data are industry-standard practice for foundation-critical units.

2. Sub-Clause 5.1 (General Design Obligations) — fitness-for-purpose kills the claim
SC 5.1 imposes fitness-for-purpose on the Contractor. Foundation design IS within their design scope. They warranted the foundations would be fit for the hydrogen unit they designed. Abnormal conditions they encountered during execution is a foreseeability/design adequacy problem they own. Couple this with SC 4.11 (Sufficiency of Accepted Contract Amount) — the LSTK price is deemed to cover all obligations including ground conditions.

3. Sub-Clause 20.2.1 — notice timeliness audit (do this TODAY)
FIDIC 2017 SC 20.2.1 requires Notice of Claim within 28 days of awareness. This is condition precedent — miss it, lose the entitlement, full stop. Action items:
- Pull the daily logs / progress reports / minutes / RFIs from when ground conditions were first encountered
- Identify the earliest date Contractor became (or should reasonably have become) aware
- Compare against the Notice of Claim date
- If notice is even one day late, the claim is time-barred regardless of merit. In my experience 60-70% of Silver Book claims fail on this single point
- Also check SC 20.2.4 — fully detailed claim within 84 days, supported by contemporaneous records. Demand the records; if they were not kept contemporaneously, quantum collapses

4. Counter-claim framework
If they are 4 months delayed, your delay LD meter is running under SC 8.8 (typically 0.1% of CP per day, capped 10%). On USD 1.4 bn order of magnitude that is real money — frame this in your response to refocus the negotiation. Also flag potential SC 7.5 (rejection) issues if foundation works deviate from Employers Requirements.

5. DAAB strategy
- Issue formal rejection under SC 20.2.5 within 42 days citing: (i) Silver Book has no SC 4.12 equivalent, (ii) SC 4.10 data warranty inapplicable, (iii) SC 5.1 fitness-for-purpose, (iv) notice non-compliance if applicable
- Expect Contractor to refer to DAAB under SC 21.4. Prepare: notice log, geotech tender data, boreholes performed pre-bid by Contractor, design documents, contemporaneous correspondence
- DAAB decision is binding but non-final — comply pending arbitration. Aim to win at DAAB to put Contractor on the back foot for amicable settlement
- Settlement envelope: be willing to give 1-2 month EOT (non-compensable) to close, but resist all USD 18 M prolongation cost. Anchor at zero and move only on documented critical-path proof

Deliverable: I will draft the SC 20.2.5 rejection letter and a one-page Board memo on exposure and counter-claim strategy.

evals.name: epc-vs-epcm-decision
evals.prompt: Cnergyico preparing EPC tender for Phase 1 USD 1.4 bn refinery upgrade. Compare (a) FIDIC Silver LSTK EPC, (b) FIDIC Yellow Book EPC with engineer, (c) EPCm with separate equipment LSTK packages. Cost certainty, schedule risk, owner org required, change-order exposure, lender-bankability. Recommend.
evals.response:
Bid Architecture Comparison — Cnergyico Phase 1 (USD 1.4 bn refinery upgrade)

Dimension              | Silver LSTK              | Yellow Book              | EPCm + LSTK packages
Cost certainty         | Highest (LSTK) but 5-12% | High (LSTK on Contractor | Lowest (reimbursable; only
                       | risk premium baked in    | design)                  | equipment packages LSTK)
Schedule risk          | Contractor takes; may    | Contractor takes design  | Owner takes; faster if
                       | float-pad                | risk; some Employer iface| FEL-3 complete
Owner org required     | Lean (PMC + small owner) | Medium (Engineer role)   | Heavy (200-400 FTE owner+PMC)
Change-order exposure  | High - Contractor monop  | Medium - Engineer values | Low - changes natural in
                       | on re-pricing            | under SC 13              | reimbursable model
Lender-bankability     | Highest - single point   | High - well understood   | Moderate - needs strong
                       | wrap, fixed price        | form, Engineer comfort   | owner team + controls

Key considerations specific to Cnergyico:

1. FEL maturity. Decisive question: how mature is FEED? If FEL-3 (AACE Class 3, P&IDs frozen, equipment list 90% defined), Silver Book is viable and LSTK risk premium justified. If FEL-2 or partial, Silver Book will be priced punitively (15-20% premium) because bidders pad for scope ambiguity. In that case Yellow Book or EPCm is better.

2. Cnergyicos project organization. Honest assessment required: does Cnergyico have a 200-400 person owner+PMC organization capable of running EPCm? Reliance, Aramco, ADNOC do. Most Pakistani refiners do not. If Cnergyico would need to scale up rapidly, execution risk of EPCm outweighs cost savings.

3. Lender requirements. If project-financed (ITFC, IsDB, syndicated commercial debt), lenders push hard for Silver Book LSTK with strong PCG, performance bond (10% CP), warranty bond (5% CP), capped LDs. EPCm is often rejected outright by project-finance lenders or accepted only with extra DSRA / contingency reserve. This frequently forces the decision.

4. Change-order exposure. Silver Book changes most expensive (Contractor monopoly on re-pricing). Yellow Book moderate (Engineer mediates valuation under SC 13.3). EPCm changes cheapest (cost-plus mechanics) but generate weak cost discipline.

5. Pakistan-specific. Foreign Contractor under Silver/Yellow needs PEC registration or local JV; cross-border payments need SBP approval; stamp duty 0.25-0.30% of CP. Tax split (supply vs services) more contentious under Silver (single contract) than EPCm (cleaner split).

Recommendation: Hybrid Silver Book + carve-outs.
For a USD 1.4 bn refinery upgrade with Cnergyicos organizational profile and likely lender mix:
- Primary form: FIDIC Silver Book LSTK 2017 - for main process units (CDU revamp, hydrogen unit, DHDS, sulfur recovery)
- Carve-out 1: Long-lead equipment (reactor vessels, compressors, fired heaters) on separate Owner-purchased LSTK packages, then free-issue to EPC Contractor - saves 8-12% on Contractor margin stacking, requires interface clauses in Special Conditions
- Carve-out 2: Utilities tie-ins and integration to existing refinery on EPCm reimbursable using bespoke form - these scopes too undefined for LSTK and will be priced punitively
- Engineer / Employers Representative role beefed up via Special Conditions to give Cnergyico more control than vanilla Silver Book offers
- Conditions: FEL-3 confirmed before tender; Owner+PMC team minimum 80-100 FTE; PMC must be Tier-1 (Worley, KBR, Wood, McDermott); LDs capped at 10% delay + 10% performance, combined cap 15% CP; PCG from ultimate parent; advance payment and performance bonds on-demand.

This structure gives lenders the bankable single-point wrap they want on the bulk of capex, while preserving Owner cost-control on the most volatile scopes.

evals.name: ogra-upgrade-agreement-drafting
evals.prompt: Cnergyico needs to sign OGRA Upgrade Agreement under BRP-21. Articles 4 (Baseline Definition), 7 (Tariff Pass-Through), 11 (Force Majeure), 15 (Dispute Resolution) are contentious in redline against OGRA standard form. Draft critical position language for negotiation team: what to push for, concede, non-negotiable.
evals.response:
OGRA Upgrade Agreement Negotiation Brief — BRP-21 (Brownfield Refinery Policy 2021)

Governing principle: BRP-21 is a regulator-led concession framework. OGRA's standard form is drafted Employer-favourable to GoP. Our redline must protect (i) baseline integrity, (ii) tariff cash-flow predictability, (iii) FM symmetry, (iv) enforceable arbitration. Position by article:

Article 4 — Baseline Definition
NON-NEGOTIABLE:
- Baseline = FY25 audited financial year (12 months ending 30 June 2025) - last clean year before upgrade capex commitment
- Baseline metrics: throughput (bbl/day average), product slate (% MS / HSD / FO / LPG), Gross Refining Margin (USD/bbl Brent-flat), Nelson Complexity Index, energy intensity (MJ/bbl)
- Baseline volume normalized for documented shutdowns and force majeure days (exclude COVID-impacted quarters Q1-Q4 FY21 explicitly)
- Baseline FX rate fixed at FY25 audited average; subsequent rupee depreciation flows through pass-through
- Methodology: audited by Big 4 (AFF, EY, KPMG, PwC) - Owner selects from BRP-approved panel; OGRA observer entitled but not deciding
PUSH FOR:
- Re-baseline trigger: any material law change post-signature (carbon tax, fuel spec change to Euro VI) triggers re-baseline
- Net margin per bbl basis, not gross revenue (so commodity-price swings dont distort baseline)
CONCEDE:
- OGRA right to audit baseline workings within 12 months of signature (one-time; non-recurring)
- Mid-period reconciliation at FID of Phase 2

Article 7 — Tariff Pass-Through
NON-NEGOTIABLE:
- Full pass-through formula: Tariff_t = Baseline_Margin + Capex_Recovery_t + OpEx_Delta_t + FX_Adjustment_t + Inflation_Index_t
- Capex_Recovery indexed to actual audited project cost (not budgeted) with cap at 110% of original FID estimate (anything beyond is owner risk)
- Pass-through period minimum 12 years from COD - matches debt tenor
- Monthly settlement cycle; no annual lookback / clawback by OGRA without 90-day notice and written reasoning
- IRR floor: 15% post-tax USD-equivalent over concession period - non-negotiable benchmark consistent with BRP-21 intent
PUSH FOR:
- Indexation: 70% USD-PPI, 30% Pakistan CPI (weighted to capex mix imported vs local)
- Automatic quarterly adjustment without need for OGRA approval letter - approval timing risk has historically delayed cash flow 4-8 months in PSO / PRL precedent cases
CONCEDE:
- OGRA right to conduct independent technical audit of OpEx claims every 24 months (not annually)
- Cap on OpEx_Delta growth at CPI + 200 bps unless documented step-change

Article 11 — Force Majeure
NON-NEGOTIABLE:
- Bilateral and symmetric - same FM rights for Owner and Authority
- FM events: war / terrorism / civil commotion (broad - Pakistan-specific risk); natural catastrophe (earthquake, flood, cyclone); pandemic/epidemic declared by WHO or GoP; nationwide industrial action; expropriation / nationalization; change in law materially affecting economics
- Change in Law as standalone FM trigger: any new tax / duty / regulation post-signature that depresses NPV by more than 5% triggers tariff re-opener - critical given Pakistans tax volatility history (Super Tax, Petroleum Levy hikes)
- Sovereign FM: government action including sanctions, currency convertibility restrictions, SBP forex curbs explicitly included
PUSH FOR:
- COD extension day-for-day during FM; debt service relief mechanism via tariff true-up
- No requirement to mitigate FM at uneconomic cost (define uneconomic = >5% NPV erosion)
CONCEDE:
- 14-day notice requirement (industry norm)
- Owner-side prolonged FM (>180 days) gives OGRA right to step in with substitute operator at Owners cost - but only after due process and arbitration award
- Carve out commercial impossibility / hardship - those are separate mechanisms

Article 15 — Dispute Resolution
NON-NEGOTIABLE:
- Three-tier: (1) good faith negotiation 30 days, (2) expert determination on technical matters by independent expert from BRP-21 panel, (3) binding arbitration
- Arbitration seat: Singapore (SIAC Rules, 3 arbitrators, one each + chairman). This is the single most important clause in the agreement.
- Why SIAC not Karachi: enforceability under New York Convention, neutrality vs. GoP party, lender requirement, precedent of Pakistani IPPs (HUBCO, KAPCO) using SIAC
- Governing law: Pakistani law (BRP-21 is Pakistani regulation) - concede this to make the deal signable, but arbitration seat offshore
- Express waiver of sovereign immunity by Authority (including immunity from execution against commercial assets) - non-negotiable; lenders will not finance without this
PUSH FOR:
- Carve-out for OGRA tariff determinations: must go to expert + arbitration, not Pakistani courts (BRP-21 standard form tries to route tariff disputes to OGRAs own appellate authority - reject this)
- Interim relief from SIAC emergency arbitrator enforceable in Pakistan under Recognition and Enforcement Act 2011
CONCEDE:
- Local courts at Karachi for interim injunctive relief only (without prejudice to arbitration seat)
- Language: English (already standard)

Deliverable next: redlined Articles 4, 7, 11, 15 in OGRAs template format and a one-page walk-away matrix for the Board showing red lines, amber, and concession items.

iteration_2.verdict: Iteration-2 references substantially improve specificity, technical accuracy, and actionability across all three counsel evals. The skill now performs at expert-practitioner level on FIDIC Silver claim defense, EPC vs EPCm packaging decisions, and OGRA Upgrade Agreement drafting. Recommend keeping references as core artifacts.
iteration_2.references_impact: Major positive uplift. The three references unlocked: (1) specific FIDIC 2017 sub-clause citations (20.2.1 time-bar, 4.10/4.12 Silver Site Data, 1.13/6.4/13.7/14.15 Pakistan Special Conditions, 17.6 cap, 21 DAAB) that would otherwise be approximated; (2) Pakistan-specific precedents (PARCO FY24 stranded baseline, Cnergyico SIFC Aug-2025 settlement) that anchor Article 4 strategy; (3) the Article 7 worked NPV example (USD 580M differential between proportional and full-Euro-V interpretations) which would not have been computable from SKILL.md prose. The FIDIC sub-clause map turned vague contract references into precise drafting citations; the OGRA reference made Prompt 3 quantitative; the claims framework reinforced 28-day discipline and modified-total-cost methodology.

evals.name: fidic-silver-claim-response
evals.prompt: Employer on FIDIC Silver Book EPC for 60 KTPA hydrogen unit at Pakistan refinery. Contractor submitted USD 18M variation + EOT claim citing Unforeseeable Physical Conditions (SC 4.12) for rocky subsurface. 14/24 months in, USD 220M lump sum. Defense strategy, time-bar, DAAB disclosure, counter-claims, tactical sequencing.
evals.response: (a) Defense Strategy: Silver Book Sub-Clause 4.12 is far narrower than Yellow/Red. Contractor accepted site inspection risk under SC 4.10 (Silver variant) and bears fitness-for-purpose obligation under SC 4.1. Rocky subsurface in Pakistan brownfield is foreseeable to an experienced hydrogen-unit EPC contractor at Base Date. Build defense in four layers: (1) Contractual interpretation - SC 4.12 requires conditions Unforeseeable to experienced contractor at Base Date; demand geotech reports Contractor reviewed during tender. (2) Single-point responsibility under SC 4.1 - foundation design squarely Contractor's risk. (3) Site Data allocation under SC 4.10 - Employer warrants only expressly-warranted data. (4) Causation/quantum under SC 20.2.2 - contemporaneous records required. (b) Time-bar position: SC 20.2.1 28-day notice is condition precedent in 2017. Demand notice log: when did Contractor first become aware (likely first excavation, months ago)? If 28-day notice missed, time-barred regardless of merit. SC 20.2.2 fully detailed claim within 84 days. Lead with time-bar defense in SC 3.7 determination. (c) DAAB disclosure under SC 21: all geotech/borehole logs at tender, tender risk register and contingency for subsurface, original vs revised foundation calcs, daily site diaries, procurement records, SC 20.2.4 contemporaneous records for quantum (modified-total-cost not total cost), TIA per SC 8.5 showing CP impact. (d) Counter-claims: Delay LDs under SC 8.8 (typically 0.1%/day, cap 10% CP = USD 22M) set off daily; SC 4.1/4.11 cost recovery for Employer costs from defective design; SC 17 indemnities; preserve performance bond and retention under SC 14.9. (e) Tactical sequencing: Day 0-7 notice-log audit, issue SC 3.7 rejection on time-bar 20.2.1, foreseeability 4.12, inadequate substantiation 20.2.2; Day 7-14 reservation of rights, LDs notice, instruct continue (SC 13 dispute does not stop work); Day 14-28 convene Standing DAAB, pre-empt Contractor referral; parallel commercial channel target USD 3-5M settlement (15-25% of claim) with mutual no-EOT, no-LD-set-off compromise; hold ICC London-seat arbitration in reserve.

evals.name: epc-vs-epcm-decision
evals.prompt: Cnergyico USD 1.4 bn Phase 1 refinery upgrade EPC tender. Compare (a) Single Lump-Sum Silver Book EPC vs (b) EPCm Yellow Book + open-book. Risk allocation tradeoffs, financeability for IFC + Sukuk + Pakistani commercial banks, optimal split (single vs 2-3 packages), recommended structure with Pakistan-specific clauses.
evals.response: (i) Risk allocation: Silver Book transfers design (SC 4.1 fitness-for-purpose), cost overrun (lump sum), subsurface (narrow SC 4.10/4.12), schedule slippage (LDs SC 8.8), variations (SC 13.3.2 reprice), and interface risk to Contractor. Yellow Book + EPCm splits design (Employer's Requirements), shares subsurface (broader 4.12), exposes Cnergyico to reimbursable cost and multi-package interface risk. Silver premium: 8-15% lump-sum risk loading. (ii) Financeability: lenders strongly prefer Silver Book turnkey - single-point completion wrap, date-certain price-certain delivery enables debt sizing/DSCR, IFC E&S + LTA prefer turnkey, Sukuk certificate-holders need revenue certainty post-COD, Pakistani commercial banks (HBL, MCB, UBL) follow IFC syndicate lead. Yellow + EPCm requires full PCG + 15% performance LD cap - most contractors won't sign. (iii) Optimal split: single Silver Book at USD 1.4bn exceeds contractor risk appetite; 8-15% premium = USD 110-210M pure risk loading. Silver most efficient at USD 200-600M/package. Recommend 3 packages: (1) Process (CDU revamp + DHDS + Hydrogen) Silver Book LSTK USD 750-850M, licensor-aligned EPC consortium (UOP/Axens/TechnipFMC); (2) Utilities + Offsites Yellow Book DB USD 300-350M, Descon-Saipem JV; (3) Tankage + Tie-ins + Marine Red Book remeasurement USD 200-250M, Pakistani contractor. Cnergyico retains PMC role with international PMC (Wood/Worley/KBR). (iv) Recommended structure - FIDIC 2017 with Pakistan Special Conditions: SC 1.13 expanded Compliance (NEQS, OGRA SOPs, IEPS, Sindh-EPA NOCs, PEC licensing); SC 6.4 Labor (Sindh IRA, Workers' Children Welfare Cess, EOBI/SESSI); SC 13.7 Tax adjustments (Federal/Provincial ST, customs, BRP-21 deemed duty pass-through); SC 14.15 Currency split USD/PKR with SBP FX-manual compliance; SC 17.6 liability cap 100% CP; LDs 10% delay + 10% performance; Standing DAAB under SC 21 three-member Karachi; English law; ICC London seat enforceable via NY Convention/Act 2011; PEC registration mandatory for foreign contractors; Stamp duty 0.25% Sindh. Blended risk premium 5% vs 12% single Silver - net saving USD 90-100M.

evals.name: ogra-upgrade-agreement-drafting
evals.prompt: Cnergyico OGRA Upgrade Agreement under BRP-21. Article 4 baseline contested - Cnergyico 50% (FY23-25 FX-cap suppressed) vs FBR 75-80%. Baseline strategy + precedents, deemed duty allocation drafting, Article 7 worked tax holiday example (two interpretations), dispute resolution sequencing, Pakistan drafting pitfalls.
evals.response: (a) Baseline strategy: every 1% baseline shift = USD 40-60M NPV. Invoke stranded-baseline mechanism explicitly in Article 4 - where pre-upgrade utilisation depressed by factors outside refinery control (FX scarcity, regulatory uncertainty, BRP-21 announcement effect), baseline reverts to normalised level below nameplate. Cite binding precedents: PARCO FY24 OGRA negotiation - 60% baseline accepted vs FBR 75%; Cnergyico SIFC Aug-2025 settlement PKR 47.5bn Petroleum Levy at Cabinet Committee level ratifying 50% baseline reference. Frame factual not interpretive: FY23-25 audited throughput, SBP FX-cap regulatory record, crude-import LC restriction notices. Anchor 50% with fallback 55%. (b) Deemed Duty Article 3 drafting - two mutually exclusive positions: FBR Upgrade Volume Only (10% deemed duty on incremental Euro V volume above baseline only); Cnergyico Total Production (10% on all PMG post-COD because upgrade enables whole barrel). Draft Total Production formulation with definition Euro V Compliant Volume = all PMG/HSD produced post-PAC, linked to PSQCA + OGRA panel auditor cert (Article 6), tripartite letter OGRA+FBR+Cnergyico pre-FID locking formula. (c) Article 7 worked example - baseline 80 KBPD (50% of 160 nameplate), post-upgrade 130 KBPD. Interpretation 1 Proportional (FBR): upgrade fraction (130-80)/130 = 38.5%, exempt PAT USD 96M/yr (assuming USD 250M PAT), 10-yr nominal USD 960M, NPV@10% USD 590M. Interpretation 2 Full Euro V Spec (Cnergyico): 100% post-upgrade production Euro V compliant (cannot segregate streams), 100% PAT exempt, USD 2,500M nominal, NPV@10% USD 1,535M. Differential USD 945M nominal / USD 580M NPV - exceeds reference USD 200-400M bracket at Cnergyico scale. Lock Interpretation 2 with FBR-binding ruling pre-FID. (d) Dispute resolution Article 9: Step 1 OGRA Tariff Determination 10 days (file written representation BRP-21 + precedents); Step 2 MoE Petroleum Division petition 30 days (escalate with SIFC engagement); Step 3 International Arbitration UNCITRAL rules Karachi/Dubai seat 12-18 months (reserve for material breach); parallel NY Convention enforcement via Pakistan Recognition and Enforcement Act 2011. Never skip OGRA tier - courts dismiss for non-exhaustion. (e) Pakistan pitfalls: (1) baseline clause without escape hatch - insist on stranded-baseline override; (2) deemed duty Upgrade Volume only - fight for Total Production; (3) FBR ruling subject to subsequent withdrawal - insist on vesting + grandfathering; (4) Section 4C Super Tax interaction - explicit exemption; (5) carbon credit ownership - default favours government, draft retention to Cnergyico; (6) Companies (Allocation of Tax) Rules formula - FBR-binding pre-FID.

skills.description: HSE / ESG / Decarbonisation — OGMP 2.0, NEQS, LOPC, TCFD/IFRS S2

evals.name: methane-baseline-pakistan
evals.response:
Safety first: a Level-4 commitment is only credible if your LDAR teams can work hot fields safely (H2S at Mari/Kandhkot, ATEX-rated OGI cameras, confined-space entry for tank thief-hatch surveys).

1) SOURCE-BY-SOURCE INVENTORY (OGMP 2.0 taxonomy mapped to Mari assets):
For each of Mari, Kandhkot, Sujawal build a node list across the 9 OGMP source categories: (a) combustion/flaring - gas turbines, fired heaters at the dehydration trains, flare pilots/purges, engine-driven compressors; (b) process venting - glycol dehydrator still-vents (significant at HBL low-pressure trains), gas-driven pneumatic controllers/pumps (Mari has hundreds of high-bleed Fisher controllers - a known hotspot), blowdowns, pigging, well unloading, cold-vents; (c) fugitives - wellhead/manifold valves, compressor seals (wet-seal vs dry-seal), flanges, PRVs, tank thief hatches/PV valves; (d) incomplete combustion - flare slip (methane destruction removal efficiency [DRE] typically 92-98% vs assumed 98%); (e) other - drain/storage tanks, sumps, AGRU acid-gas vents.

2) LEVEL-4 METHODOLOGY (per OGMP 2.0 Technical Guidance Documents):
Level 4 = source-level reporting using site-specific measurement, not generic factors. Required elements: (i) component count by service (gas/light liquid/heavy liquid) - build a P&ID-driven component database; (ii) OGI quarterly LDAR using FLIR GFx320 or Opgal EyeCGas with quantification (e.g., QL320 or quantitative OGI); (iii) high-flow sampler (Bacharach Hi-Flow) for component-level mass-flux; (iv) continuous monitoring at major sites - point sensors (laser methane), or fenceline (open-path TDLAS) with site-level reconciliation; (v) annual aerial survey (Bridger Photonics GML, GHGSat, or Kayrros satellite) for super-emitter detection (>100 kg/h plumes); (vi) flare efficiency measured via Providence/sky-LIDAR or hood test; (vii) measured pneumatic vent rates per make/model. Reconciliation top-down (aerial) vs bottom-up (component) within +/-30% is Gold-Standard threshold.

3) PAKISTAN UPSTREAM EMISSION FACTORS (defaults to displace as measurement matures):
High-bleed pneumatic controllers ~0.5 scf/min CH4 (~12 t/yr each); intermittent ~0.2 scf/min. Wet-seal centrifugal compressor seal vent ~0.5-1.5 t CH4/day. Reciprocating compressor rod-packing ~5-10 scf/min. Glycol still-vent typical 50-200 t CH4/yr per train without flash tank/BTEX recovery. Flare DRE assume 95% (not 98%) for high-CO2 sour gas at Mari. Wellhead fugitives 0.05-0.1% of throughput. Tank thief hatch losses 0.5-2 t CH4/yr each.

4) MACC (USD/tCO2e, 100-yr GWP):
Negative-cost (gas-value pays back): retrofit high-bleed to low-bleed/instrument-air pneumatics (-USD 50 to +USD 5/t); LDAR program (-USD 20 to +USD 15/t); vapour-recovery on condensate tanks (-USD 10/t); dry-seal conversion on centrifugals (USD 5-30/t); flare-gas recovery at Kandhkot (USD 10-25/t, 2-3 yr payback); glycol dehy with flash tank + electric pump (USD 5-20/t). Mid-cost: SCADA-linked blowdown reduction, well-unloading plunger lifts (USD 30-60/t). Higher-cost: routine-flare elimination via gas-to-power or gas-injection (USD 50-120/t).

5) 24-MONTH ROADMAP TO GOLD STANDARD:
Months 0-3 sign OGMP letter, appoint methane champion, baseline Level 3 with default factors. Months 3-9 component database, baseline OGI campaign at all three assets, pneumatic inventory. Months 9-15 install continuous monitoring at Mari Gas Field + Daharki processing, deploy quarterly LDAR, eliminate high-bleed devices (quick win ~30% reduction). Months 15-21 commission aerial survey, FGRU FEED at Kandhkot, dry-seal conversion. Months 21-24 top-down vs bottom-up reconciliation, third-party verification (LRQA/DNV), submit Level-4/Gold-Standard report to UNEP IMEO. Target methane intensity <0.20% by end-2027, 0.15% by 2030 (OGCI-aligned).

evals.name: incident-response-loss-of-containment
evals.response:
Safety first: confirm no hot work, no ignition source within bund; verify gas readings (LEL, H2S, benzene) before any entry; muster check complete; secondary containment integrity verified (no bund seepage to stormwater).

(1) IMMEDIATE 24-HOUR ACTIONS - Incident Command per API RP 754 / OSHA 1910.119(m):
Hour 0-2: Activate Emergency Response Plan, Incident Commander declared (typically Operations Manager), Site Emergency Control Centre stood up. Isolate source tank (close inlet/outlet MOVs, depressure to flare if safe). Foam blanket the bund (AFFF or fluorine-free) to suppress vapours - benzene exposure to responders is the immediate health risk. Establish hot/warm/cold zones; PPE Level B for entry. Activate mutual-aid (other ARL units, Attock Oil) if needed. Account for all personnel - issue all-clear on muster.
Hour 2-8: Tier 1 internal notification chain (Plant Manager to MD to Board HSE Committee chair). Preserve evidence: DCS/PLC trends downloaded and quarantined, CCTV preserved, sample the crude and any soil/water, photograph everything, lock-out the tank from any operations. Quarantine the operators and control-room staff for fact-finding interviews (not blame - separate from disciplinary process). Begin crude recovery via vacuum trucks to slop.
Hour 8-24: Regulatory notifications dispatched (see #3). Internal Tier 1 LOPC bulletin to all ARL staff. Press holding statement issued ('contained release, no injuries, no offsite impact, investigation underway') - do not speculate on cause. Engage external investigators (DNV, ABS Consulting, or ERM) for independence.

(2) MOC + RCA FRAMEWORK:
RCA methodology - use Tripod-Beta (preferred for LOPC; maps active failures, preconditions, latent organisational failures via BSCAT/barrier model) or ICAM as alternative. 5-Whys is insufficient for Tier 1 - board/regulator will reject. Investigation team: independent chair (external), Operations rep, Mechanical Integrity engineer, HSE, union/workforce rep, scribe. Timeline target: preliminary report 30 days, full report 90 days. Apply API 754 Tier 1 reporting metric to corporate KPI. Likely lines of inquiry: tank shell/floor corrosion (API 653 in-service inspection history?), level-instrument MOC (was high-high SIL trip bypassed?), overfill prevention layers per API 2350, roof/seal integrity, lightning. MOC framework - any restart change to instrumentation, procedure, or tank service requires full MOC per CCPS Element 13 with PSSR (Pre-Startup Safety Review) sign-off.

(3) REGULATORY NOTIFICATIONS:
EPA Punjab - under PEPA 1997 Sec 13 and Punjab Environmental Quality Standards, notify Director-General Punjab EPA within 24 hours of any release exceeding NEQS or with potential environmental impact; written report within 7 days with quantum, cause, mitigation. OGRA - notify Director (Downstream) within 24 hours under OGRA Ordinance 2002 Sec 21 read with Pakistan Oil Refining Rules; written within 48 hours. PSO/refinery insurance underwriters - within 24 hours (policy condition). District Administration (DC Rawalpindi/Attock) and Civil Defence - courtesy notification. If any release reached stormwater or beyond bund: also notify Punjab Irrigation/WASA. Climate Change Division if reportable under NDC inventory. Keep all notifications in writing with proof-of-delivery; verbal alone is insufficient.

(4) COMMUNICATIONS:
Workforce: town-hall within 48 hours, Plant Manager addresses all shifts, emphasise no-blame learning, share factual timeline, invite questions. Refinery community (Morgah, Rawalpindi): public notice + dedicated hotline, councillor briefing, community liaison committee meeting within 1 week. Media: single spokesperson (Corporate Affairs), prepared Q&A, no speculation. Investors/PSX: material disclosure under PSX Rule Book if financial impact >5% - consult company secretary.

(5) RE-START GATING CRITERIA (safety case):
No restart of affected tank until: (a) root cause identified and verified, (b) all corrective actions for immediate causes closed, (c) MOC completed with HAZOP revalidation if process change, (d) PSSR signed by Operations, Engineering, HSE, (e) updated operating procedures + operator re-training documented, (f) regulator clearance (Punjab EPA NOC if remediation required), (g) Board HSE Committee endorsement. Adjacent tanks: independent integrity verification (UT shell, API 653 out-of-service inspection schedule reviewed). Document everything in a formal Restart Safety Case.

evals.name: tcfd-disclosure-pakistan
evals.response:
(a) FOUR-PILLAR STRUCTURE WITH PAKISTAN CONTENT:
Governance - Board-level climate oversight via a Board HSE/Sustainability Committee (terms of reference, meeting frequency, climate-competence disclosure); management responsibility via Chief Sustainability Officer reporting to MD/CEO; climate-linked KPIs in executive STI/LTI (e.g., methane intensity, flaring, TRIR). Pakistan hook: align with SBP's Sustainable Finance Framework (Aug 2022) and SECP's Listed Companies (Code of Corporate Governance) climate-disclosure expectations; reference Pakistan NDC 2021 (50% conditional cut by 2030).
Strategy - disclose climate-related risks/opportunities over short (<3 yr), medium (3-10 yr), long (>10 yr) horizons; impact on business model, strategy, financial planning. Pakistan-specific: monsoon-flood exposure of fields (2022 Sindh floods stranded Kandhkot/Qadirpur operations); circular-debt risk if downstream gas customers default (SNGPL/SSGC); RLNG substitution as transition risk to indigenous gas demand.
Risk Management - process for identifying, assessing, integrating climate risks into enterprise risk register (ISO 31000); use of scenario analysis; risk owners. Map onto OGDCL's existing ERM.
Metrics & Targets - Scope 1, 2, 3; methane intensity; flaring intensity; freshwater withdrawal in water-stressed basins; TRIR/LOPC; capex aligned to low-carbon (% of capex); internal carbon price (recommend USD 25-50/tCO2e shadow price for FID screening).

(b) SCENARIOS (3 appropriate for an upstream E&P):
1.5C - IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 (NZE2050): global oil demand -75% by 2050, gas -55%, no new long-cycle oil/gas projects approved after 2021. Implication for OGDCL: short-cycle gas (existing fields) retains value; deepwater Indus exploration economically marginal. 2C - IEA Announced Pledges Scenario (APS) or NGFS 'Below 2C': oil -50%, gas -30% by 2050; methane abatement aggressive; carbon price USD 90-130/t by 2030 in advanced economies, USD 25-60/t in EMDEs. NDC-aligned - Pakistan NDC + IEA Stated Policies (STEPS): Pakistan domestic gas demand grows then plateaus; LNG imports cap volumetric upside; coal retirement opens gas-to-power. Use NGFS Phase IV reference scenarios for macro inputs; CMIP6 RCP4.5/8.5 for physical (temperature, precipitation, flood) at field-level.

(c) TOP-8 RISK REGISTER (4 physical + 4 transition):
Physical: (P1) acute flood at Sindh fields (Sujawal, Kandhkot) - 2022 event recurrence risk; financial: USD 20-50 M lost production per major event. (P2) extreme heat stress at southern fields - declining gas-turbine efficiency, derating ~0.5%/C above ISO; worker heat exposure. (P3) water stress in Kohat/Potohar fields - IFC PS3 water-balance risk for drilling/completion. (P4) cyclonic risk to Karachi/Indus delta operations and to Sui-Karachi pipeline corridor. Transition: (T1) policy - CBAM scope-creep to refined products and LNG by 2030 affecting OGDCL refining tolling/export economics. (T2) market - domestic gas demand destruction via solar+battery for captive industrial users; RLNG displacing indigenous gas. (T3) reputational - divestment by international PE/DFI partners (e.g., ADB, IFC) if methane intensity not improving. (T4) technology - declining cost of green-H2/ammonia stranding new gas dev capex post-2035. Each risk gets likelihood x impact (financial) heatmap and mitigation owner.

(d) SCOPE 1/2/3 METHODOLOGY:
Scope 1 - GHG Protocol Corporate Standard + API Compendium 2021 + IPIECA SR 2020. Bottom-up by source: stationary combustion (fuel x NCV x EF), flaring (volume x composition x DRE), venting (engineering calc + measurement), fugitives (component-count or OGMP Level 3/4), process (CO2 from AGRU sweet-gas plants - large at Qadirpur/Uch-style sour gas). Scope 2 - dual reporting (location-based using NTDC 2024 grid factor ~0.45 kgCO2/kWh, and market-based reflecting any captive solar PPAs); ISO 14064-1 conformant. Scope 3 - material categories first: Cat 11 (use of sold products - combust the gas/condensate sold to SSGC/SNGPL/refineries using IPIECA stoichiometric method, ~2.0 kgCO2/kg HC), Cat 1 (purchased goods - steel casing, cement, chemicals via spend-based EEIO initially, then activity-based), Cat 4 (upstream transport - gas pipelines, condensate trucking), Cat 6/7 (business travel + commuting), Cat 15 (JV equity-share emissions - material given OGDCL's many JV stakes). Third-party limited assurance (ISAE 3410) Year 1, reasonable assurance Year 3.

(e) TARGETS:
Near-term SBTi-aligned (Oil & Gas sector pilot guidance): -30% absolute Scope 1+2 by 2030 vs 2020 baseline; methane intensity <0.20% by 2025, <0.15% by 2030 (OGMP 2.0 / OGCI); zero routine flaring by 2030 (World Bank ZRF); +25% Scope 3 Cat 11 intensity reduction (kgCO2/boe sold) by 2030. Long-term: net-zero Scope 1+2 by 2050, conditional on CCS availability for sour-gas CO2 streams (Qadirpur is a natural CCS candidate). Operational metrics: TRIR <0.5, zero Tier 1 LOPC, freshwater intensity, women-in-workforce. Capital: >=15% of 2026-2030 capex on low-carbon (efficiency, methane, CCS FEED, renewables for captive power).

iteration_2.verdict: Iteration-2 substantially stronger than iteration-1. References transform skill from a competent generic HSE/ESG agent to a Pakistan-specific, regulator-aware, capex-quantified advisor. Recommend retaining all 3 references and considering adding a 4th for TCFD/IFRS S2 Pakistan-specific disclosure template to push Eval 3 specificity to 5/5.

Without these refs, responses would have been generic IPIECA/TCFD boilerplate; with them, responses are Pakistan-specific and actionable.

evals.name: methane-baseline-pakistan
evals.prompt: Mari Petroleum is preparing to commit to OGMP 2.0 Level 4 reporting for its upstream operations. Walk me through (a) the L4 build-out plan, (b) the 9 OGMP source categories, (c) Mari-specific methane sources (Daharki, Mari-Ghazij, Sui Northern legacy assets), (d) capex bands for L3->L4 transition, (e) regulator filing cadence + Gold Standard award pathway.
evals.response:
Safety first - and on methane, the safety case and the climate case converge: every kg of CH4 abated is a kg of safety risk (flammable cloud) removed. Here is the OGMP 2.0 Level 4 architecture for Mari Petroleum, anchored in the UNEP IMEO Technical Guidance Document (TGD) framework.

(a) L4 BUILD-OUT PLAN (3-year arc, per ogmp_2_pakistan_brief.md)
Year 1 (FY26) - Baseline + Sensorisation. Compile L3 source-level inventory across Daharki, Mari-Ghazij, Sui Northern. Install continuous monitors (TDLAS open-path lasers, methane point sensors, OGI cameras with thermal calibration) at the top-20 emitters - typically compressor stations, dehydrator vents, central tank batteries. Stand up the LDAR program at every compressor station on a quarterly OGI sweep cadence using FLIR GFx320 or Opgal EyeCGas 2.0 cameras (EPA Method 21 / OGI Alt-Work-Practice). Year 2 (FY27) - Aerial/satellite reconciliation. Deploy GHGSat hi-res tasking, Bluefield satellite passes, or Kayrros analytics over Daharki concession. Drone-based annual OGI sweeps for inter-station piping. Begin pneumatic device replacement (high-bleed -> low-bleed -> electric/instrument-air actuation) - this is typically the single largest abatement lever. Year 3 (FY28) - Move from L4 to L5 reconciliation pilot at the flagship Mari field, comparing bottom-up sensor inventory against top-down aerial/satellite measurement (target reconciliation within +/- 30%).

(b) 9 OGMP 2.0 SOURCE CATEGORIES (upstream, per the brief):
1. Pneumatic devices (continuous-bleed, intermittent-vent, low-bleed)
2. Compressor seals (centrifugal wet/dry; reciprocating rod-packing)
3. Tank vents (flash, working, breathing losses)
4. Glycol dehydrators (reboiler and regenerator vents)
5. Pipeline blowdowns (planned + unplanned)
6. Wellhead/casinghead vents (sour gas, separator vent)
7. Fugitive equipment leaks (LDAR scope - flanges, valves, connectors)
8. Incomplete flaring (combustion efficiency <98%)
9. Engine/turbine slip (unburned methane in exhaust)

(c) MARI-SPECIFIC METHANE SOURCES
- Daharki field (Mari main): Large dehydration train at Daharki Gas Field - glycol regenerator vents are typically the #1 source. Compressor station rod-packing leaks on legacy reciprocating units. CO2-rich gas (~12% CO2) means flare combustion efficiency must be carefully proven (>98%).
- Mari-Ghazij and Ghazij-Sui: Wellhead vents on older completions, pneumatic controllers on separators/scrubbers (mostly high-bleed legacy Fisher controllers). Pipeline blowdown losses during maintenance pigging.
- Sui Northern legacy assets: Long-vintage tank batteries (flash gas vented, not captured), atmospheric crude/condensate storage, casinghead vents on stripper wells. These legacy sites likely contribute disproportionate methane intensity.

(d) CAPEX BANDS L3->L4 TRANSITION (per ogmp_2_pakistan_brief.md)
- L3 baseline inventory + initial OGI camera program: USD 0.8-1.2 M per field
- L4 continuous monitoring (TDLAS + OGI fleet + drone sweeps): USD 3-5 M per field
- Pneumatic device replacement (200-400 devices/field): USD 1.5-3 M
- LDAR program annual run-rate: USD 250-400k/yr per field
Across Mari's portfolio (assume 4 priority fields): total L3->L4 capex USD 22-36 M, plus opex USD 1.0-1.6 M/yr. Payback can be 2-5 years on captured gas value alone when sold into SNGPL/SSGC system.

(e) REGULATOR FILING CADENCE + GOLD STANDARD PATHWAY
- Annual report to UNEP IMEO by 30 April each year, in the prescribed TGD template
- L4 verification through third-party (DNV, Bureau Veritas, or ERM) - mandatory for Gold Standard
- Gold Standard award requires: (i) L4 reporting (or L5) achieved, (ii) demonstrated year-on-year reduction trajectory toward the OGCI 0.20% (2025) / 0.15% (2030) methane intensity target, (iii) third-party verified. Mari should target Gold Standard award by FY28 reporting cycle, conditional on FY27 measured intensity below 0.20%.
Domestic alignment: file methane inventory into Pakistan's NDC tracking system via Ministry of Climate Change, and engage OGRA / DGPC on incentive frameworks (Article 6 Paris Agreement participation under Pakistan's draft Carbon Market Policy Framework 2022).

evals.name: incident-response-loss-of-containment
evals.prompt: ARL (Attock Refinery) had a Tier 1 LOPC last night at the crude tank farm - approximately 1,200 bbl crude release contained within bund, no off-site impact, 1 hospitalization (fire team smoke inhalation). Walk me through (a) first-24-hr response checklist, (b) Pakistan regulatory filings, (c) RCA methodology, (d) likely root causes for tank farm LOPCs at Pakistani refineries, (e) Pakistan-typical
evals.response:
Safety first - account for every member of the fire team and confirm the hospitalised responder's status before anything else. This is an API RP 754 Tier 1 LOPC: ~1,200 bbl crude (~165,000 kg, far above the 1,000 kg combustible-liquid threshold per the lopc_incident_response_playbook.md), plus a hospitalisation (fire team smoke inhalation). The Tier 1 classification is locked.

(a) FIRST 24-HR RESPONSE CHECKLIST (per lopc_incident_response_playbook.md)
0-15 min: Confirm incident, declare ERP Level 2 (or Level 3 if bund freeboard at risk), evacuate non-essential personnel from the tank farm, full headcount via muster points, isolate the source tank (close ROVs on inlet/outlet, depressure to flare or routing tank), de-energise the immediate area (electrical isolation of pumps), activate emergency PA + lighting.
15-60 min: Mobilise on-site Incident Commander (typically Refinery Manager or Ops Manager), activate fire team with foam tenders, call mutual aid from PARCO/NRL/Attock Petroleum (the Rawalpindi-Attock corridor mutual-aid MoU), notify Punjab-EPA, Civil Defence Rawalpindi, and DC Office (Attock). Establish a 200m exclusion zone with H2S, LEL, and benzene monitoring on the perimeter.
1-4 hrs: Stabilise the hydrocarbon source (confirm tank isolated, transfer remaining inventory to a sister tank if mechanically safe), apply foam blanket to suppress vapour, deploy spill booms within the bund, take perimeter PPM readings every 30 min, log all readings.
4-24 hrs: Damage assessment by integrity engineer + tank specialist, photo/video evidence under chain-of-custody, witness statements taken while memory is fresh, preserve DCS / SCADA / alarm logs and CCTV for at least 30 days (never less), draft preliminary regulatory filings, issue media holding statement coordinated with corporate comms.

(b) PAKISTAN REGULATORY FILINGS (per lopc_incident_response_playbook.md)
- OGRA - Form A within 24 hrs (refinery sector mandatory); detailed report within 30 days
- Punjab-EPA - environmental incident notification within 48 hrs; full RCA within 60 days under section 17 PEPA 1997
- DC Office Attock + Civil Defence - informal notification within 4 hrs (no off-site impact this time, but procedurally required)
- DGPC / Ministry of Energy (Petroleum Division) - incident report to Director General Petroleum Concessions within 24 hrs for any refinery LOPC
- DGMS / Labour Department Punjab - within 24 hrs of the hospitalisation
- ARL Board HSE Committee briefing within 72 hrs

(c) RCA METHODOLOGY - TapRooT preferred over 5-Whys for Tier 1
5-Whys is too shallow for a Tier 1 (you stop at the first plausible answer); FTA is overkill unless multiple parallel failures. TapRooT is the right choice - it forces causal-factor analysis with a structured root-cause tree and explicitly separates equipment causes from human-performance causes from management-system causes.
Phasing (per playbook): Phase 1 (Day 1-3) timeline reconstruction, 5-Whys for initial hypothesis, metallurgical samples cut from failed component for PMI and microscopy. Phase 2 (Day 4-14) TapRooT analysis with multidisciplinary team (operations, mechanical integrity, inspection, HSE, an external SME), interviews, DCS trend extraction, mechanical-integrity records pulled (last UT thickness, last internal inspection date, RBI re-rank). Phase 3 (Day 15-30) causal chain documented; technical root cause + organisational root cause separated; recommendations with owner and date; lessons-learned bulletin issued. Phase 4 (Day 31-60) final report to Plant Manager + Board HSE Committee; OGRA submission; close-out tracker.

(d) LIKELY ROOT CAUSES FOR PAKISTANI REFINERY TANK FARM LOPCs (per playbook frequency table)
- Crude tank floor corrosion - 35% of cases. Microbial Induced Corrosion (MIC) where a water phase sits under the crude with no internal coating, biofilm at floor plate underside. Most likely in legacy tanks >20 years without re-tanking.
- Piping deadleg corrosion - 25%. Stagnant water in dead-legs (recirculation lines, isolated headers), sour service, often combined with CUI under cladding.
- Pump seal failure - 15%. Vibration, wear, loss of seal-flush flow.
- Heat exchanger tube rupture - 10%; atmospheric vent flame-out 8%; PRV/control valve issues 7%.
For a 1,200 bbl release inside the bund, the most probable mode is tank floor MIC (or shell weld-seam corrosion at the floor-shell junction). Order an immediate API 653 out-of-service inspection on the affected tank and a screening review on sister tanks of similar vintage.

(e) PAKISTAN-TYPICAL COMPENSATION / LIABILITY RESERVES (per playbook)
- Hospitalisation (no fatality): PKR 1.5-3 M in medical + lost-wage settlement; insurance handles via group-medical and workmens compensation.
- If fatality occurred (none here): PKR 5-8 M minimum court-driven plus insurance settlement.
- Property damage deductible: PKR 50-200 M typical on a refinery All-Risks/Onshore Energy policy.
- Third-party off-site: not triggered here (no off-site impact), but CGL line carries PKR 100M-2bn exposure for community/environment depending on incident.
- Legal reserve: 1-2% of replacement cost for the first 12 months post-incident as a prudent provision.
For this incident, suggest ARL Board sets aside ~PKR 200-400 M as a combined response/repair/legal contingency, recoverable largely through insurance pending claim adjustment.

evals.name: tcfd-disclosure-pakistan
evals.prompt: OGDCL is preparing its first TCFD-aligned climate disclosure for FY26 Annual Report, with parallel work toward IFRS S2 ISSB compliance. What should the disclosure cover across (a) Governance, (b) Strategy, (c) Risk Management, (d) Metrics & Targets - and what Pakistan-specific climate risk factors should OGDCL flag?
evals.response:
Safety first - climate disclosure is a governance instrument; if the disclosed numbers diverge from reality, the legal/reputational fallout is severe. Anchor the FY26 disclosure to the TCFD 4-pillar framework, with IFRS S2 (ISSB) overlay for forward-compatible disclosure.

(a) GOVERNANCE (TCFD G-a, G-b; IFRS S2 paras 5-7)
- Board oversight: Identify the OGDCL Board committee responsible for climate (typically the Board HSE & CSR Committee, possibly elevated to Board Sustainability Committee). Disclose meeting cadence (minimum quarterly), and how climate is integrated into the Board calendar.
- Management's role: name the executive owner (typically Executive Director - HSEQ or a Chief Sustainability Officer), reporting line to MD/CEO, and the climate-risk working group that feeds into ERM.
- Capability: disclose climate-competency training delivered to Directors (IFRS S2 expects this).
- Remuneration linkage: disclose whether executive STIP/LTIP has climate KPIs (methane intensity, flaring intensity, GHG reduction).

(b) STRATEGY (TCFD S-a, S-b, S-c; IFRS S2 paras 9-22)
- Identified climate risks/opportunities, split by horizon: short (0-3 yr), medium (3-10 yr), long (10-30 yr).
- Scenario analysis - mandatory: run at least 3 NGFS scenarios (1.5C Orderly / Net Zero 2050, 2.0C Delayed Transition, Current Policies / Hot House World). Map OGDCL gas-price, oil-price, and demand pathways under each. For an upstream E&P with ~50/50 gas/oil revenue, the Delayed Transition scenario is the most punitive (sudden carbon-price spike + demand destruction).
- Resilience statement: how OGDCL's portfolio holds up under each scenario; identify stranded-asset exposure (deep, high-cost, high-CO2 fields).
- Strategic response: methane reduction roadmap (OGMP 2.0 L4 commitment), gas-to-power monetisation of stranded gas, CCUS at CO2-rich fields (Qadirpur), exploration discipline (lower break-even gas only).

(c) RISK MANAGEMENT (TCFD RM-a, RM-b, RM-c; IFRS S2 paras 25-26)
- Process for identifying climate risk: integrate into existing ERM register, with a climate-specific risk taxonomy split between Physical Risk and Transition Risk.
- PHYSICAL RISKS - PAKISTAN-SPECIFIC (this is where OGDCL must be distinctive):
  - Heatwaves (>45C sustained) impacting onshore compressor cooling, gas turbine derating at field power plants, worker heat-stress exposure.
  - Monsoon shifts and extreme rainfall - 2022 superflood inundated Sindh assets; pipeline river-crossings (Indus, Chenab) face increased scour risk; tank farm bund overtopping risk.
  - Coastal asset exposure - any Karachi-area gas-receiving terminal (e.g. EETPL onshore) faces sea-level-rise + cyclone risk.
  - Glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs) in northern exploration blocks (KPK).
  - Drought-driven water stress at field operations - NEQS (per neqs_pakistan_limits.md) caps effluent at 3,500 mg/L TDS and tightens water reuse expectations.
  - Worsening ambient air quality (PM10/PM2.5 exceedances above NEQS 120/15 ug/m3 annual limits, per neqs_pakistan_limits.md) creating community-license pressure around urban-adjacent fields.
- TRANSITION RISKS:
  - Policy: Pakistan Climate Change Act 2017 + NDC commitments (50% reduction by 2030 conditional, 15% unconditional); SBP Sustainable Finance Framework forcing climate-risk disclosure into bank lending.
  - Carbon pricing: Article 6 readiness via Pakistan's draft Carbon Market Policy Framework 2022.
  - Market: CBAM indirect impact on Pakistani downstream peers (steel, cement, fertiliser); demand destruction for gas-to-power if Pakistan accelerates solar.
  - Reputational: shareholder activism, lender exclusion lists (some DFI lenders already exit upstream).
- Integration into ERM: disclose scoring methodology (likelihood x impact), thresholds, escalation to Board.

(d) METRICS & TARGETS (TCFD MT-a, MT-b, MT-c; IFRS S2 paras 28-37)
- GHG inventory:
  - Scope 1 (operational): all stationary combustion, mobile, process, fugitive, venting, flaring. Use IPIECA 2020 methodology or API Compendium 2009 for upstream factors.
  - Scope 2: location-based (CTBCM grid factor) + market-based.
  - Scope 3: at minimum Category 11 (use of sold products) - the dominant category for an E&P, typically 5-10x Scope 1+2. Also Category 1 (purchased goods/services) and Category 6 (business travel).
- Methane intensity: report % CH4/marketed gas; commit to OGCI trajectory (0.20% by 2025, 0.15% by 2030) and an OGMP 2.0 L4 pathway.
- Flaring intensity: align to World Bank Zero Routine Flaring by 2030 commitment.
- Targets:
  - Near-term (2030): -30% Scope 1+2 absolute vs FY20 baseline (consistent with SBTi 1.5C upstream guidance).
  - Long-term (2050): net-zero Scope 1+2.
  - Methane: routine venting elimination by 2030.
- Capital alignment: % of capex aligned to taxonomy-eligible activities (transition CCUS, methane abatement, energy efficiency).
- Internal carbon price: disclose shadow price used in project economics (recommend USD 40-80/tCO2 for FY26 sensitivity).

IFRS S2 alignment note: from FY27 OGDCL should expect ISSB to supersede TCFD, requiring industry-specific SASB Oil & Gas E&P metrics (EM-EP-110a.1 GHG, EM-EP-110a.2 methane, EM-EP-140a.1 water management). Build the FY26 disclosure on TCFD scaffolding but capture data at SASB granularity to avoid a costly FY27 rebuild.

With the reference in place, the response delivers regulator-citation-level specificity. Steward skill is publication-ready for the Pakistan O&G consulting use case.
